repo_notes.author: ArcKit maintainers
repo_notes.content: AUTHORITATIVE OVERVIEW — before generating anything, read `docs/book/ARCKIT-BOOK.md`. It is the canonical, maintainer-written description of ArcKit and supersedes any inference drawn from file structure alone. Next, read `CLAUDE.md` (project instructions) and `README.md` (quick start + distribution overview). Prefer paraphrasing from these three files over reconstructing meaning from code. The page hierarchy in this file mirrors the book's part/chapter structure (Parts I–IV) so that every wiki page corresponds to a book chapter.

repo_notes.author: ArcKit maintainers
repo_notes.content: CURRENT SIZE — 68 officially-maintained slash commands (the baseline quoted in marketing). From v4.7.0 an optional EU/FR community overlay adds 18 more, for 86 total when enabled. Always cite 68 as the top-line; the extra 18 are counted inside their own community section. ArcKit also ships 10 autonomous research agents, 4 skills, 5 MCP servers, and 62 document templates. Earlier auto-generated wikis cited 57 commands and 6 agents — those numbers are stale.

repo_notes.author: ArcKit maintainers
repo_notes.content: SEVEN DISTRIBUTION FORMATS (not four) — (1) Claude Code plugin `arckit-claude/` (canonical), (2) Gemini CLI extension `arckit-gemini/`, (3) Codex CLI extension `arckit-codex/`, (4) OpenCode CLI extension `arckit-opencode/`, (5) Copilot prompt pack `arckit-copilot/`, (6) Python CLI `src/arckit_cli/` used to scaffold Codex/OpenCode/Copilot projects, (7) TypeScript `paperclip` plugin (72 tools, committed on branch). Older wiki generations listed only four — that is outdated.

repo_notes.author: ArcKit maintainers
repo_notes.content: SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH — `arckit-claude/commands/` and `arckit-claude/agents/` are canonical. All other formats are generated by `scripts/converter.py` via an `AGENT_CONFIG` dictionary. The converter rewrites `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` paths, inlines agent prompts for platforms without Task/subagent architecture, strips Claude-only frontmatter fields (`effort`, `maxTurns`, `disallowedTools`, `initialPrompt`, `paths`, `keep-coding-instructions`), generates Codex `config.toml` and per-agent TOML, and emits Gemini TOML. Never describe extension directories as independent implementations — they are build outputs. Edits belong in `arckit-claude/` and in the converter.

repo_notes.author: ArcKit maintainers
repo_notes.content: ORANGE BOOK RISK MODEL — `/arckit.risk` implements HM Treasury Orange Book (2023) risk management. The Orange Book defines SIX treatment options, not the popular '4Ts' (Tolerate / Treat / Transfer / Terminate). ArcKit's risk command uses the correct six-option model together with the Three Lines of Defence and cascade analysis. An earlier version described '4Ts'; that was a factual error caught by ArcKit's own autoresearch system and corrected. Do not describe the risk command as using '4Ts' or as having four treatment options.

repo_notes.author: ArcKit maintainers
repo_notes.content: GREEN BOOK BUSINESS CASE — `/arckit.sobc` generates a Strategic Outline Business Case following HM Treasury Green Book (2026) five-case structure: Strategic, Economic, Commercial, Financial, Management. Use the Green Book's own vocabulary: `BAU` (not 'status quo'), `Do Minimum` (not 'baseline'), `Preferred Way Forward` (not 'recommended option'). Do not rename these terms.

repo_notes.author: ArcKit maintainers
repo_notes.content: PLATFORM REQUIREMENTS — the Claude Code plugin requires Claude Code v2.1.112 or later (current floor, bumped in v4.6.7). Plugin and CLI versions track independently: CLI 4.7.1, plugin 4.7.1. `scripts/bump-version.sh` updates 15 version files in one pass (was 13 before Paperclip). Earlier wiki generations cited 11 version files and Claude Code v2.1.71 — both stale.

repo_notes.author: ArcKit maintainers
repo_notes.content: WARDLEY MAPPING — the Wardley suite is five commands, not one: `wardley.value-chain`, `wardley`, `wardley.doctrine`, `wardley.climate`, `wardley.gameplay`. The main `wardley` command emits dual output (OWM syntax for create.wardleymaps.ai plus Mermaid `wardley-beta` blocks). Validated against 147 real-world maps from Simon Wardley's public repository with 98% pass rate; 100% of ArcKit-generated syntax is valid (the 3 failing maps are source-data errors in the originals). Do not describe Wardley support as a single command.

repo_notes.author: ArcKit maintainers
repo_notes.content: GOVERNMENT CODE DISCOVERY — for UK Government projects, three commands use the `govreposcrape` MCP server over 24,500+ UK public-sector repositories: `/arckit.gov-code-search`, `/arckit.gov-reuse`, `/arckit.gov-landscape`. These feed into the main research command as a fifth build-vs-buy option ('Reuse existing government code'). Distinct from cloud-vendor research commands (`aws-research`, `azure-research`, `gcp-research`) which use AWS Knowledge MCP, Microsoft Learn MCP, and Google Developer Knowledge MCP.

repo_notes.author: ArcKit maintainers
repo_notes.content: WHAT ARCKIT IS NOT — ArcKit is not a diagramming tool (like Structurizr or ArchiMate), a requirements database (like Jama or DOORS), or a wiki. It is a prompt-driven governance automation system that uses AI coding assistants as document-generation engines, guided by 50–200-line prompt files stored as Markdown with YAML frontmatter. Differentiators: template-driven consistency, traceability by construction (every command reads upstream artifacts), real-time MCP-backed research (not training-data lookups), multi-platform distribution from one source, and a self-improving autoresearch system that optimises prompts against real government framework content.

pages.title: Overview
pages.purpose: Introduce ArcKit as a prompt-driven enterprise architecture governance toolkit: what it generates, who uses it, and how it differs from diagramming, requirements, and wiki tools.

page_notes.content: Mirror the framing in Chapter 1 of `docs/book/ARCKIT-BOOK.md`: the problem is scattered artifacts with broken traceability; the solution is AI-assisted template-driven document generation with enforced cross-references. Quote the 'at a glance' numbers (68 commands, 10 agents, 17 hooks, 4 skills, 5 MCP servers, 7 distribution formats, 62 templates, 22 test repos). Emphasise that the commands are prompt files, not code.

pages.title: What is ArcKit?
pages.purpose: Explain the governance problem ArcKit solves (scattered Word/SharePoint/Confluence artifacts with no traceability) and the five things that make ArcKit different.

page_notes.content: Use the five differentiators from Chapter 1: (1) template-driven consistency, (2) traceability by construction, (3) real-time MCP-backed research (not training-data lookups), (4) multi-platform from one source, (5) self-improving autoresearch. Do not describe ArcKit as a 'tool' in the sense of Structurizr or DOORS — it is a prompt library that runs inside AI coding assistants.

pages.title: System Architecture
pages.purpose: Describe the hub-and-spoke architecture: Claude Code plugin as canonical source, converter.py as the build tool, six generated distribution formats.

page_notes.content: Make the hub-and-spoke model explicit: edits happen in `arckit-claude/commands/` and `arckit-claude/agents/`; `scripts/converter.py` regenerates the other six formats. Cover: path rewriting (`${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` → platform-specific), agent inlining for platforms without subagents, frontmatter stripping (Claude-only fields removed), and per-platform config generation (Codex `config.toml`, Gemini TOML, Copilot prompt files). Do not imply the extensions are hand-maintained.

pages.title: Seven Distribution Formats
pages.purpose: List and compare all seven distribution formats with install method, feature matrix, and target audience for each.

page_notes.content: Formats and install methods: Claude Code plugin (marketplace `tractorjuice/arc-kit`), Gemini CLI extension (`gemini extensions install tractorjuice/arckit-gemini`), Codex CLI extension (`tractorjuice/arckit-codex`), OpenCode CLI extension (scaffolded by `arckit init --ai opencode`), Copilot extension (`arckit init --ai copilot`), Python CLI (`pip install arckit-cli` or `uv tool install`), Paperclip TypeScript plugin (on branch, 72 tools). Claude Code is the 'premier experience' with hooks, agents, and bundled MCP. Other platforms get inlined agents and omit hooks. Do not say four platforms — that is outdated.

pages.title: Getting Started
pages.purpose: Get a new user from zero to running their first ArcKit command, covering install, onboarding, and the recommended first workflow.

page_notes.content: Follow Chapter 2 of the book. Entry point for new users is `/arckit.start` on Claude Code or `arckit init` for CLI-based platforms. The recommended first sequence is: principles → stakeholders → risk → SOBC → requirements. Emphasise that Claude Code needs no project init (plugin works in-place), while Codex and OpenCode require `arckit init` to scaffold `.arckit/`, `.codex/` or `.opencode/`, and `projects/`.

pages.title: Installation
pages.purpose: Cover install methods for each of the seven distribution formats, with prerequisites and verification steps.

page_notes.content: Claude Code plugin: `/plugin marketplace add tractorjuice/arc-kit` then enable `arckit`. Requires Claude Code v2.1.112 or later. Plugin updates come automatically from the marketplace. Gemini: `gemini extensions install tractorjuice/arckit-gemini`. CLI: `pip install arckit-cli` or `uv tool install arckit-cli`. For Codex/OpenCode/Copilot, after installing the CLI run `arckit init <project> --ai <codex|opencode|copilot>` to scaffold the project. Do not invent package names — only the ones listed here exist.

pages.title: First Workflow
pages.purpose: Walk through a complete example from initial `/arckit.start` through the first five governance artifacts.

page_notes.content: Use the example session in Chapter 2 of the book. The five foundational artifacts are generated by: `/arckit.principles`, `/arckit.stakeholders`, `/arckit.risk`, `/arckit.sobc`, `/arckit.requirements`. Each command reads the outputs of previous commands — requirements cite stakeholder goals, SOBC benefits trace back to stakeholder goals, risks link to the RACI matrix. Output filenames follow `ARC-{PROJECT_ID}-{TYPE}-v{VERSION}.md`. Principles are global (`ARC-000-PRIN-v1.0.md` in `projects/000-global/`); everything else is project-scoped.

pages.title: Choosing an AI Platform
pages.purpose: Help users pick between the seven distribution formats based on feature needs, existing tooling, and sandbox/security constraints.

page_notes.content: Use this rule of thumb: Claude Code for the most feature-complete experience (hooks + agents + MCP + monitors). Gemini CLI for sandboxed environments with bundled MCP but no agents. Codex / OpenCode / Copilot for organisations standardised on those IDEs. Python CLI if you need project scaffolding for Codex/OpenCode/Copilot. Paperclip if you need ArcKit exposed as a tool in a browser-native workflow. Hooks are Claude-only; agents are Claude-only but inlined as direct prompts on other platforms.

pages.title: The Governance Lifecycle
pages.purpose: Present ArcKit's commands as a phased governance lifecycle following the book's Part II structure — Foundation through Compliance — and explain the dependency structure matrix.

page_notes.content: Use Chapter 3 of the book. The 68 commands are organised into 16 tiers (Foundation → Strategic Context → Risk → Business Justification → Requirements → Strategic Planning → Detailed Design → Procurement → Design Reviews → Implementation → Backlog Export → Operations & Quality → Compliance → Reporting → Publishing). Commands in higher tiers depend on artifacts from lower tiers. Five pre-defined workflow paths: Standard (4–8mo), UK Gov (6–12mo), UK Gov AI (9–18mo), MOD Defence (12–24mo), MOD Defence AI (18–36mo). Dependencies have three strengths: Mandatory, Recommended, Optional.

pages.title: Foundation: Principles, Stakeholders, Risk, Business Case
pages.purpose: Document the four foundational commands that every project starts with — /arckit.principles, /arckit.stakeholders, /arckit.risk, /arckit.sobc — and the traceability chain they establish.

page_notes.content: Traceability chain: Stakeholders → Drivers → Goals → Outcomes → RACI; each risk links to a RACI-assigned stakeholder; every SOBC benefit traces back to a stakeholder goal. Principles are technology-agnostic (characteristics, not products) and stored globally at `ARC-000-PRIN-*`. Risk command uses Orange Book SIX treatment options (NOT '4Ts') plus Three Lines of Defence plus cascade analysis. SOBC uses Green Book five-case model with required vocabulary (BAU, Do Minimum, Preferred Way Forward).

pages.title: Requirements and Design
pages.purpose: Cover the requirements and detailed-design commands: /arckit.requirements, /arckit.data-model, /arckit.data-mesh-contract, /arckit.dpia, /arckit.dfd, /arckit.adr, /arckit.diagram, /arckit.glossary.

page_notes.content: Requirements taxonomy uses five prefixes: BR (business), FR (functional), NFR (non-functional, with sub-prefixes like NFR-P performance and NFR-SEC security), INT (integration), DR (data). Requirements are the most-connected artifact in the dependency matrix — they feed research, data model, procurement, design, backlog, and compliance commands. Bidirectional traceability is explicit: downstream artifacts cite 'Source Requirements: DR-001, DR-002, FR-015' and 'Implements Decision: ARC-001-ADR-005'. ADRs use MADR v4.0. Diagrams use C4 model with Mermaid output.

pages.title: Strategy and Wardley Mapping
pages.purpose: Cover the strategic-planning commands and the full Wardley mapping suite: /arckit.roadmap, /arckit.strategy, /arckit.platform-design, /arckit.framework, and the five wardley.* commands.

page_notes.content: Wardley suite is FIVE commands (wardley.value-chain, wardley, wardley.doctrine, wardley.climate, wardley.gameplay) — never describe it as a single command. Main `wardley` command emits dual output: OWM syntax for create.wardleymaps.ai and Mermaid `wardley-beta` blocks for GitHub rendering. Doctrine covers 40+ principles across 4 phases. Climate covers 32 patterns in 6 categories. Gameplay covers 60+ strategic patterns with D&D alignment categorisation. Evolution stages: Genesis (0.00–0.25), Custom (0.25–0.50), Product (0.50–0.75), Commodity (0.75–1.00). Validated on 147 real Simon Wardley maps with 98% pass rate.

pages.title: Market Research and Knowledge Compounding
pages.purpose: Cover the research agents — /arckit.research, /arckit.datascout, /arckit.aws-research, /arckit.azure-research, /arckit.gcp-research, /arckit.gov-reuse, /arckit.gov-code-search, /arckit.gov-landscape, /arckit.grants — and explain how research artifacts feed downstream commands.

page_notes.content: Research commands are Claude-only agents (inlined as direct prompts on other platforms) that perform 10+ WebSearch/WebFetch/MCP calls in an isolated Task subprocess, keeping that context out of the main conversation. Cloud research uses dedicated MCP servers: AWS Knowledge, Microsoft Learn, Google Developer Knowledge. Gov research uses the `govreposcrape` MCP server over 24,500+ UK public-sector repositories — this feeds `/arckit.research` as a FIFTH build-vs-buy option ('Reuse existing government code'). Research artifacts go stale at 6 months per the health command's STALE-RSCH rule.

pages.title: Procurement and Vendor Evaluation
pages.purpose: Cover procurement commands: /arckit.sow, /arckit.dos, /arckit.gcloud-search, /arckit.gcloud-clarify, /arckit.evaluate, /arckit.score.

page_notes.content: UK public-sector procurement context is explicit: G-Cloud (for commodity cloud services) and DOS (Digital Outcomes and Specialists, for custom services). `/arckit.score` is unique in ArcKit because it writes structured JSON (`docs/scores.json`), not Markdown — each scoring session appends to the existing data, the command supports sensitivity analysis, and a PreToolUse hook (`score-validator.mjs`) validates JSON structure before every write. `/arckit.evaluate` produces a technology evaluation matrix; `/arckit.sow` produces a Statement of Work for vendor RFPs.

pages.title: Design Reviews
pages.purpose: Cover the two design-review commands — /arckit.hld-review and /arckit.dld-review — their decision framework, and how conditional approvals are tracked.

page_notes.content: Two-tier design review: HLD (High-Level Design) validates system architecture and strategic alignment; DLD (Detailed-Level Design) validates technical specifications and operational readiness. Reviews evaluate: requirements coverage, principles compliance, security/compliance (NCSC frameworks for UK Gov), technical quality, operational readiness. The UNRESOLVED-COND health rule flags reviews marked 'APPROVED WITH CONDITIONS' that have no resolution evidence — this keeps conditional approvals from becoming silent technical debt.

pages.title: Delivery and Operations
pages.purpose: Cover delivery and operations commands: /arckit.backlog, /arckit.story, /arckit.trello, /arckit.servicenow, /arckit.devops, /arckit.mlops, /arckit.finops, /arckit.operationalize, /arckit.presentation, /arckit.pages.

page_notes.content: These commands turn governance artifacts into operational reality. `/arckit.backlog` generates a product backlog from requirements. `/arckit.story` generates user stories. `/arckit.servicenow` generates ServiceNow CMDB configurations. DevOps / MLOps / FinOps commands produce maturity assessments. `/arckit.pages` produces an interactive HTML dashboard at `docs/index.html` — users re-run it after updates to regenerate. `/arckit.presentation` produces a MARP slide deck.

pages.title: Compliance and Quality Assurance
pages.purpose: Cover compliance commands (/arckit.tcop, /arckit.secure, /arckit.mod-secure, /arckit.jsp-936, /arckit.ai-playbook, /arckit.atrs, /arckit.conformance, /arckit.maturity-model, /arckit.service-assessment, /arckit.principles-compliance) and quality commands (/arckit.analyze, /arckit.traceability, /arckit.impact, /arckit.search, /arckit.health).

page_notes.content: UK Gov compliance set: TCoP (Technology Code of Practice), Secure by Design (NCSC CAF), MOD Secure by Design (JSP 453), JSP 936 (MOD AI safety), AI Playbook, ATRS (Algorithmic Transparency Recording Standard), GDS Service Assessment. Quality tools: `/arckit.health` scans for seven stale/orphan patterns (STALE-RSCH, FORGOTTEN-ADR, UNRESOLVED-COND, ORPHAN-REQ, MISSING-TRACE, VERSION-DRIFT, STALE-EXT) and writes `docs/health.json` for the dashboard. `/arckit.traceability` generates end-to-end requirement-to-artifact mapping. `/arckit.impact` performs reverse-dependency analysis (blast radius) for proposed changes.

pages.title: Inside ArcKit
pages.purpose: Present the internal systems that make ArcKit work — Part III of the book — covering prompt engineering, agents, hooks, skills, templates, conversion, and autoresearch.

page_notes.content: This section is for readers who want to understand or extend ArcKit, not just use it. Mirror Part III of the book. Each child page documents one internal system. Consistent pattern across these pages: what the system is, why it exists, where the files live, and what developers need to know to modify it.

pages.title: Commands and Prompt Engineering
pages.purpose: Document the anatomy of an ArcKit command: YAML frontmatter, prompt body structure, the seven recurring prompt patterns, effort levels, the handoffs schema, and the conversational-gathering pattern.

page_notes.content: Every command is a Markdown file with YAML frontmatter and a 50–200-line prompt body. Frontmatter fields: `description`, `argument-hint`, `effort` (low/medium/high/max/xhigh), `handoffs` (suggested next commands), `tags`, `keep-coding-instructions` (persists across /compact, v2.1.94+). The seven recurring patterns in command bodies: (1) Project Context Hook Awareness, (2) Template Reading with User Override, (3) Prerequisite Checking, (4) External Document and Policy Scanning, (5) Citation Traceability, (6) Write Tool for Large Documents, (7) Quality Checklist Verification. Conversational gathering is capped at 2 AskUserQuestion rounds to prevent endless clarification loops.

pages.title: The Agent System
pages.purpose: Document the 10 autonomous agents, the thin-wrapper delegation pattern, agent frontmatter fields, and how agent context is isolated from the main conversation.

page_notes.content: 10 agents currently: arckit-research, arckit-datascout, arckit-aws-research, arckit-azure-research, arckit-gcp-research, arckit-framework, arckit-gov-reuse, arckit-gov-code-search, arckit-gov-landscape, arckit-grants. Agents exist when a command performs >10 WebSearch/WebFetch/MCP calls — isolating that context in a Task subprocess keeps the main conversation clean. Valid agent frontmatter in plugin context: `name`, `description`, `model`, `effort`, `maxTurns`, `disallowedTools`, `initialPrompt`. Fields like `color`, `permissionMode`, `tools` are invalid in plugin context. Agents are Claude-only — other platforms get the agent prompt inlined by `scripts/converter.py`.

pages.title: The Hook System
pages.purpose: Document all 17 registered hooks, the hook events they subscribe to, the `if:` matcher syntax, and how hooks eliminated 20–50 Read operations per command.

page_notes.content: Hooks are Claude-only. Registered in `arckit-claude/hooks/hooks.json`. Supported events include SessionStart, UserPromptSubmit, PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop, StopFailure, PermissionRequest, plus newer PostCompact (v2.1.76), FileChanged / CwdChanged / TaskCreated (v2.1.83–84), PermissionDenied (v2.1.89), and PreCompact blocking (v2.1.105). Hooks use an `if:` field (v2.1.85+) with permission-rule syntax (e.g. `Write(/projects/**)`, `Bash(git *)`) to narrow triggering and avoid Node process spawns. Key hooks: SessionStart injects version context, UserPromptSubmit scans project context (eliminates boilerplate Read calls), PreToolUse validates filenames, PostToolUse regenerates traceability. 18 handler files total — 17 registered, 1 unwired.

pages.title: Skills, MCP Servers and References
pages.purpose: Document the 4 skills, 5 MCP servers, user-config wiring, the shared references directory, and the guide system.

page_notes.content: Skills live in `arckit-claude/skills/{name}/SKILL.md`. Description cap is 1,536 chars (raised from 250 in v2.1.105). `paths:` field (v2.1.84+) scopes auto-activation. 5 MCP servers: AWS Knowledge, Microsoft Learn, Google Developer Knowledge, Data Commons, govreposcrape. User config in `arckit-claude/.claude-plugin/plugin.json` under `userConfig` key (v2.1.83+): GOOGLE_API_KEY and DATA_COMMONS_API_KEY (sensitive, stored in keychain), plus organisation_name, default_classification, governance_framework. Runtime substitution via `${user_config.KEY}`; converter rewrites to `${KEY}` for non-Claude extensions. References directory holds citation-instructions.md and the Quality Checklist shared across commands.

pages.title: The Template and Document System
pages.purpose: Document the 62 templates, the Document Control standard, the ARC document ID system, requirement ID prefixes, the template override pattern, and citation traceability.

page_notes.content: Document ID format: `ARC-{PROJECT_ID}-{TYPE}-v{VERSION}.md`. Project IDs are zero-padded (001, 002) and extracted from `projects/NNN-project-name/` directories. Every template starts with a Document Control block (14 standard fields: Document ID, Type, Project, Classification, Status, Version, Created/Last Modified Date, Review Cycle, Next Review Date, Owner, Reviewed By, Approved By, Distribution) followed by Revision History and a standard footer. Template override pattern: commands check `.arckit/templates-custom/` first, fall back to `.arckit/templates/` — customisations survive `arckit init --here` upgrades. Citation format: `[DOC_ID-CN]` inline, with Document Register + Citations table in an 'External References' section of every artifact.

pages.title: The Multi-AI Converter
pages.purpose: Document `scripts/converter.py` in detail: the AGENT_CONFIG dictionary, path rewriting, agent inlining, frontmatter stripping, per-platform config generation, and skill command rewriting.

page_notes.content: `scripts/converter.py` is config-driven — adding a new AI target requires a new `AGENT_CONFIG` dict entry. Converts `arckit-claude/commands/*.md` into all configured output formats: Codex Markdown (`.codex/`), Codex Skills (`arckit-codex/skills/arckit-*/` with `SKILL.md` + `agents/openai.yaml` + `allow_implicit_invocation: false`), OpenCode Markdown (`.opencode/`), Gemini extension TOML (`arckit-gemini/`), Copilot prompt files (`arckit-copilot/`). Per-target transformations: rewrite `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` paths, inline agent prompts, strip Claude-only frontmatter fields, generate Codex `config.toml` (MCP + agent roles), generate per-agent TOML with `developer_instructions`, rewrite skill references (`/arckit:X` → `/prompts:arckit.X` for Codex).

pages.title: The Autoresearch System
pages.purpose: Document the autoresearch system that iteratively optimises command prompts against real government framework content — the experiment loop, two-layer evaluation, and real examples of errors it caught.

page_notes.content: Autoresearch runs overnight, iterating prompt versions and scoring them against real source documents. Two-layer evaluation: layer 1 scores the generated artifact for framework coverage; layer 2 verifies framework claims against the actual source (gov.uk, NCSC, etc.). Real discoveries include: the Orange Book '4Ts' error (actual Orange Book has 6 treatment options — corrected), and Green Book terminology (BAU / Do Minimum / Preferred Way Forward rather than generic business-case vocabulary). All 48 autoresearched commands were consolidated in PR #265 in early 2026.

pages.title: Development and Operations
pages.purpose: Document ArcKit's own development lifecycle — how the project is versioned, released, tested, and maintained.

page_notes.content: Mirror Part IV of the book. The three key topics are release management (with scripts), the test-repo fleet (22 real-world projects on GitHub used as regression tests), and highlights from the commit history.

pages.title: Version Management and Releases
pages.purpose: Document the version model (CLI and plugin tracked independently, extensions tracking the plugin), the 15 version files, release scripts, and the GitHub release automation.

page_notes.content: 15 version files (not 11 — that is stale). Source of truth: `VERSION` (CLI) and `arckit-claude/VERSION` (plugin). Extensions track plugin version. Release scripts: `scripts/bump-version.sh <version>` updates all 15 files in one go; `scripts/generate-release-notes.sh [prev-tag]` parses git log into Keep-a-Changelog markdown (auto-detects previous tag); `scripts/push-extensions.sh` pushes extension directories to their separate GitHub repos. `.github/workflows/release.yml` creates a GitHub Release on `v*` tag push. Development rule: every change goes through a feature branch and PR — never push directly to main.

pages.title: Test Repositories and Real-World Examples
pages.purpose: Document the 22+ public and private test repositories used as regression tests and real-world demonstrations — what they cover, which are public, and how they use the plugin.

page_notes.content: Test-repo pattern: `arckit-test-project-v*`. 47 numbered test repos currently (v0–v46, though v12 is retired and v46 has duplicates). Public examples include NHS Appointment Booking (v7), HMRC Tax Assistant (v2), Cabinet Office GenAI (v9), Scottish Courts GenAI (v14), UK Fuel Price Transparency (v17), UK Government API Aggregator (v19). Since 2026-02-07 all test repos use the plugin via marketplace instead of synced files — each has a `.claude/settings.json` that auto-enables `arckit@arc-kit`. What stays in repos: `projects/` (generated artifacts), `docs/`, repo-specific `README.md`/`CLAUDE.md`. Plugin provides commands, agents, templates, scripts, MCP servers — no file syncing needed.

pages.title: Commit History Highlights
pages.purpose: Narrate the key milestones in ArcKit's evolution — the Multi-Platform Moment, the Plugin Pivot, the Hook Revolution, the Wardley Suite, Government Code Discovery, the Citation Traceability release, and the GitHub Trending moment.

page_notes.content: Follow Chapter 13 of the book. Key milestones: v0.3.0 (Oct 2025) SOBC + risk added; v2.0.0 (7 Feb 2026) Plugin Pivot away from synced files; v4.3.0 (16 Mar 2026) Wardley Suite; v4.5.0 (23 Mar 2026) Government Code Discovery; v4.6.0 (24 Mar 2026) self-improving prompts via autoresearch; v4.6.3 (6 Apr 2026) Citation Traceability; v4.6.4 (7 Apr 2026) 68th command; 19 Apr 2026 GitHub Trending daily. Do not narrate roadmap or unreleased features as if they were shipped — only milestones present in the actual commit history.

pages.title: Reference and FAQ
pages.purpose: Provide the command reference, document type code registry, hook reference, glossary, and frequently asked questions — all consolidated lookup tables from the book's appendices.

page_notes.content: Sources: Appendices A (Complete Command Reference), B (Document Type Code Registry), C (Hook Reference), E (Glossary), F (FAQ) of `docs/book/ARCKIT-BOOK.md`. Present the command list grouped by the same phase order as the Governance Lifecycle pages so readers can jump between overview and reference. Do not invent new type codes or command names — if a fact is not in the appendices, do not include it.
